note_data.title: Crime and Deviance
keyPoints: **Crime** is behaviour that breaks the formal written law and is punishable by the state through the criminal justice system
**Deviance** is behaviour that goes against the norms and values of society but may not necessarily be illegal
**Social control** operates through formal agencies (police, courts, prisons) and informal agencies (family, peer groups, media)
**Relative nature** means what is considered criminal or deviant varies between cultures, time periods and social groups
**Labelling theory** suggests individuals become deviant when society labels them as such, leading to self-fulfilling prophecy
**White-collar crime** refers to crimes committed by people in professional or business positions, often involving financial fraud
**Punishment** serves four main functions: deterrence, retribution, rehabilitation and public protection
**Age, gender and social class** are key factors that influence patterns of crime and criminal behaviour
**Media representation** of crime can create moral panics and amplify deviance in society

definitions.definition: Behaviour that breaks the formal written law and is punishable by the state through official sanctions
Behaviour that goes against the accepted norms and values of society and results in negative sanctions
The methods used by society to encourage conformity and prevent deviant behaviour through formal and informal agencies
The process by which society attaches negative labels to certain individuals or groups, potentially leading to further deviance
Criminal acts committed by people in professional, managerial or business positions, typically involving financial or corporate fraud

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give arguments for AND against with evidence and examples
Evaluate strengths and weaknesses, conclude with overall judgement

modelAnswer.question: Explain why women commit fewer crimes than men. [6]
modelAnswer.answer: Women commit fewer crimes than men because of different socialisation patterns. Girls are socialised to be more conformist and less aggressive than boys, making them less likely to engage in criminal behaviour. Women also face greater informal social control through family responsibilities and social expectations of respectability. Additionally, women have fewer opportunities to commit certain types of crime, particularly violent crimes, due to their generally smaller physical size and different social roles. The chivalry factor also means that women may be treated more leniently by the criminal justice system, leading to lower recorded crime rates.
markBreakdown: Mark 6: Chivalry factor in criminal justice

commonMistakes: Never say 'deviance is always illegal' — deviance can be legal behaviour that breaks social norms
Students often confuse formal and informal social control — formal uses official agencies, informal uses social pressure
Don't assume all criminals are from working class backgrounds — white-collar crime shows middle class criminality
Avoid saying 'crime rates are rising everywhere' — crime patterns vary by type, location and time period
Never ignore the relative nature of crime — what is criminal varies between societies and over time
examinerTips: This topic appears frequently in Paper 2 Section B questions worth 15 marks
Always use Pakistani examples like honour crimes, corruption cases, or traffic violations to show application
Link crime and deviance to other topics like social stratification and education for higher level analysis
Remember to distinguish between different types of crime (violent, property, white-collar) in longer answers
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Crime and deviance overlap diagram showing how some deviant acts are criminal (theft), some criminal acts are not deviant to all groups (tax evasion), and some deviant acts are not criminal (unusual dress)
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q5 (8 marks) — 'Growth in global crime has been caused by capitalist exploitation of developing societies.' Evaluate this view.
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — 'Globalisation has made crime harder to police.' Evaluate this view.
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q3 (4 marks) — 'Poverty in developing societies is the main factor contributing to the growth in global crime.' Evaluate this view.
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q2 (4 marks) — Describe two examples of cyber crimes.
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P1 Q6 (6 marks) — Explain how victim surveys are used to measure crime.

workedExample.question: Explain how victim surveys are used to measure crime. (6 marks)
steps: Step 1: Define victim surveys - large-scale questionnaires asking representative samples about their experiences of crime
Step 2: Explain the methodology - surveys like the British Crime Survey ask people about crimes they've experienced, whether reported to police or not
Step 3: Identify what they reveal - uncover the 'dark figure' of crime by including unreported incidents
Step 4: Give specific examples - domestic violence, minor theft, and sexual offences are often underreported to police but revealed in victim surveys
Step 5: Explain limitations - rely on memory, may exclude certain groups (homeless, institutionalized), and can't measure victimless crimes
workedExample.answer: Victim surveys are large-scale questionnaires conducted on representative samples of the population to gather information about their experiences of crime. The most well-known example is the British Crime Survey, which asks people about crimes they have experienced over the past year, regardless of whether they reported these incidents to the police. This methodology helps uncover the 'dark figure' of crime - incidents that don't appear in official statistics. Victim surveys are particularly valuable for revealing crimes that are often underreported, such as domestic violence, minor theft, and sexual offences, providing a more complete picture of crime levels than police records alone. However, they have limitations including reliance on victims' memory, potential exclusion of certain groups like the homeless or institutionalized populations, and inability to measure victimless crimes like drug use.
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for defining victim surveys; 2 marks for explaining methodology with examples; 2 marks for evaluation of strengths and limitations

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused crime with deviance throughout their answers' — always distinguish between crime (law-breaking) and deviance (norm-breaking) when analyzing social control

Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently provided only Western examples when discussing global crime' — always include examples from developing societies and transnational criminal networks when evaluating globalization's impact

Examiner Report 2022: 'Weaker responses failed to link theoretical perspectives to contemporary crime patterns' — always connect functionalist, Marxist, or interactionist theories to modern examples like cybercrime or corporate crime
note_data.keyDiagram: The Crime Triangle Diagram: Draw a triangle with three points labeled 'Offender', 'Target/Victim', and 'Location/Opportunity'. Connect these with arrows showing how crime occurs when all three elements intersect. Add surrounding factors: social controls (formal/informal), environmental design, and guardianship. Include examples at each point - motivated offender (unemployment, peer pressure), suitable target (wealthy area, vulnerable person), and lack of capable guardian (poor lighting, no CCTV). This demonstrates routine activity theory and situational crime prevention approaches.
note_data.quickRecap: Crime violates laws while deviance breaks social norms - they overlap but aren't identical. Official statistics undercount crime due to unreported incidents, revealed through victim surveys and self-report studies. Globalization creates new criminal opportunities (cybercrime, human trafficking) while making enforcement harder across borders. Different theoretical perspectives explain crime patterns: functionalists see crime as inevitable and boundary-maintaining, Marxists link it to capitalism and inequality, while interactionists focus on labeling processes and social construction of deviance.
